posts.text:
📊 The 4 Paradoxes of AI Developer Productivity

- The "More Code, Less Software" Paradox

🧠 What we think: More AI-generated code = more features shipped.

📉 What data shows: CircleCI analyzed 28M CI workflows and found average throughput surged 59% YoY, but median main-branch throughput dropped 7%. Almost none of the feature-branch explosion reached production. Recovery times hit a 5-year high (72+ min). 

Source: CircleCI 2026 State of Software Delivery Report

- The "Faster to Write, Longer to Review" Paradox

🧠 What we think: AI saves hours per week on coding tasks.

📉 What data shows: Faros AI found that for every PR merged with AI, code review time increased 91%. The time saved writing code is completely re-absorbed by auditing and verification. 

Source: Faros AI Engineering Report 2026 (10,000+ devs)

- The "47% More Workflows, Same Output" Paradox

🧠 What we think: More productivity activity = more business value delivered.

📉 What data shows: CircleCI found that even top-10% teams, ie running 47% more workflows, achieved essentially flat main-branch activity. Feature-branch throughput jumped 50%, but almost none translated into shipped changes. 

Source: CircleCI & Thoughtworks 2026

- The "84% Use It, 96% Don't Trust It" Paradox

🧠 What we think: Universal adoption means validated, reliable output.

📉 What data shows: Sonar's 2025 State of Code survey found 72% of developers use AI tools daily, estimating 42% of committed code is AI-generated, yet 96% don't fully trust AI output. DORA reports 30% explicitly distrust AI-generated code. 

Source: Sonar 2025 & Google DORA 2025


AI isn't the problem. 

Treating it as a coding shortcut instead of a systems challenge is.


Sources for further reading:

1. CircleCI 2026 State of Software Delivery - 28 million CI workflow analysis showing throughput-up-shipment-down paradox: https://lnkd.in/dQv3m-Rd

2. Thoughtworks' breakdown of the CircleCI report, Martin Fowler's team explains why the bottleneck moved and how to respond: https://lnkd.in/dVdWy_i2

3. Faros AI Engineering Report 2026 - 22,000+ engineers reveal review time up 91%, incidents more than double: https://lnkd.in/dW9E7CuB

4. Google DORA 2025 Report - AI amplifies both strengths and dysfunctions; throughput improves while stability deteriorates: https://lnkd.in/d-Kpx_U3

5. Sonar 2025 State of Code - 42% of committed code is AI-assisted, 96% of devs don't fully trust AI output: https://lnkd.in/dYYWZxY6

posts.text:
In spring 2022, a small group of us from SEB’s cloud transformation visited Spotify’s headquarters to learn how they do FinOps at scale. 

We came away impressed, inspired – and I left with one line I’ve never forgotten.

Our host, Anders Hagman, and his team showed us how they practiced FinOps across thousands of engineers, with all workloads in the cloud. 

One thing was particularly surprising though! 

They weren’t (only) counting stacks of dollars or euros saved. 

Instead, they translated their optimisations and savings into the number of new colleagues Spotify could hire as a direct consequence of their work.

I found that extremely human-centric, and deeply aspirational.

The reasoning was simple: 

Spotify’s primary growth constraint (at the time) wasn’t technology – it was an insufficient number of skilled, talented people, in turn limited by financial constraints. 

By reframing cloud savings into people they could now afford to bring on board, FinOps became instantly relatable. 

Not a spreadsheet exercise. 

A talent multiplier.

Fast forward to today. 

Our world is now dominated by AI models, tokens and prompts. 

Cost per 1k tokens, GPU utilisation, inference vs. training spend – the metrics have become less human centric. 

And I wonder:

Is there now a similar ambition to save in on unnecessary Cloud and AI costs voiced at Spotify, or anywhere else, so that more colleagues can be brought onboard? 

Or does that phrasing belong to a time that has gone by?

In an era obsessed with unit economics of intelligence, maybe the most powerful FinOps metric is still the one Anders left with me four years ago: 

How many more dreamers, builders and problem-solvers can we afford to hire because we optimise our Cloud and AI costs?

#FinOps #CloudOptimisation #AIOptimisation #HumanCentric #AI #Talent #EngineeringCulture

posts.text:
I’ve always been a fan of Uncle Bob’s teachings on what an architect should be, and I’ve always seen all my engineers as architects in their own right. 

An architect is not necessarily a job title but a state of mind and operation.

That mindset becomes even more crucial when you weave it into today’s AI developments. 

AI tools can churn out code at incredible speed, but the real architectural work, the thinking, the boundary drawing, the ownership of consequences, still belongs to the hands‑on developer. 

Here’s how that philosophy fits the AI era:

- AI amplifies the need for architectural judgement, it does not replace it. 

A developer who has stepped back from the code can’t tell whether an AI’s suggestion respects the system’s dependency rules or introduces hidden coupling. Only someone wiring in every day, feeling the friction, can sense when the design is drifting.

- The architect becomes the ultimate reviewer and integrity guard.
  
Bob Martin says architects must suffer the consequences of their choices. Now you have to suffer the AI’s choices too. That means reviewing, testing, and refactoring what the tool produces, so you don’t delegate understanding to a black box.

- Design intent must be expressed as living code.

The AI matches patterns; it doesn’t grasp the trade‑offs you’ve intentionally made. So you encode your architectural boundaries in tests, interfaces, and module structures that guide the tool’s output. That act of framing is pure architecture, and it demands hands‑on presence.

- The “architect as the strongest programmer” now includes AI literacy.

To lead by example, you must wield AI tools with the same discipline you’d apply to hand‑written code: prompt thoughtfully, reject anything you can’t explain, and never let generated code that you don’t understand into the codebase.

- The risk isn’t mediocre diagrams, it’s architectural erosion at machine speed.
  
Without a coding architect, a team leaning heavily on AI can fork from the intended design in days. Staying immersed means you spot the small divergences before they become a rewrite.

So if you already treat every engineer as an architect, then AI simply raises the stakes: the state of mind that insists on taking responsibility for the system’s structure is the only thing that stops the project from becoming a pile of plausible‑looking but incoherent pieces. 

That’s Uncle Bob’s philosophy in the AI age, the architect’s hands stay dirty, the whole team owns the design, and the tool stays a tool, never the decision‑maker.

#UncleBob #SoftwareArchitecture #CleanArchitecture #HandsOnArchitect #ArchitectsCode #AIandCoding #DeveloperMindset #ArchitectStateOfMind #CodeOwnership #AILiteracy #DesignIntegrity #EveryEngineerAnArchitect #CodingIsCraft #ResponsibleAI

posts.text:
X: SaaS is dead.

Me: Oh dear. I suppose your argument is it's quicker to build your own than use a SaaS?

X: Exactly. You disagree?

Me: There's a slight problem. 

X: ?

Me: SaaS was a marketing term.

X: Explain.

Me: Activities evolve from genesis to custom built to product to commodity. When we talk about services then they start to appear in custom built as timeshare, in product as rental and in commodity as utility services. SaaS was used as a catch all for all of these in the application space but timeshare, rental and utility are not the same. There are solid utility services out there. An easy example of this can be found in the infrastructure space with EC2 (compute as utility). In the software application space you have a mixed bag of some utility like services and then a whole bunch of things with providers who have not been industrialising and they've been exploiting ecosystems of consultants to customise. To be blunt, they've been pretending timeshare or rental models are utility models and they deserve to feel some pain. So, some SaaS is dead but that is not the same as all SaaS is dead.

X: How can I tell the difference?

Me: Try the cheat sheet, attached.

X: Why don't I just get an AI to build it all for me?

Me: There's no point in rebuilding highly industrialised utility services unless you are a glutton for punishment. Sure, there are lots of monstrous deathstar projects out there which are faking it by trying to be the platform for everything and are far from tightly defined utility services. Personally, I'd spend 30 minutes or so mapping out the space and then use that to determine what utility services you can use and where you might legitimately want to rebuild.

X: Seems easier to just get an AI to build it all for me.

Me: Well, I suppose the good news is that some LLM/GPTs have even been spontaneously providing Wardley Maps in PRDs they create. In any case your LLM/GPT will recommend using some services (which are utility like) and not others because they have a bias towards whatever is the most common, well documented path for a given problem (a useful but imperfect proxy). If this continues, they might start doing the "thinking" for you. That's not a bad thing given you seem so keen not to spend 30 minutes on this.

X: So, SaaS is dead?

Me: Timeshare and rental models faking it as a utility with large consultant ecosystems might face some trouble ... yes. 

X: So, SaaS is dead?

Me: Why do I feel I might have a better conversation talking with your LLM/GPT?

X: I'm not interested in mapping stuff, I just want an answer!

Me: It's not binary. Ask your LLM/GPT ... oh, actually, don't bother. Just ask your "AI" to build your stuff and let it deal with the nuance.

X: So, I'm right? Let AI build it all, SaaS is dead?

Me: FFS. Sure, just when your LLM/GPT recommends using Vercel, SendGrid, Stripe and S3, just go along with it.

posts.text:
Over the years I’ve been back and forth on the need for prescriptive and opinionated CI/CD toolchains. 

I’ve seen both highly centralized and decentralized implementations across different companies, and I’ve come to view this as one of the more consequential, yet under-discussed, architectural decisions in platform engineering.

In my experience, highly centralized implementations raise the floor for all feature teams. 

They ensure build and deployment consistency while enabling governance and security at a scale that is otherwise impossible. 

When all company deployments go through a centrally managed CI/CD toolchain, getting metrics, implementing guardrails, and ensuring SBOM compliance is considerably easier than a free-for-all, "pick your own adventure" approach.

The obvious drawback? 

The team in charge of this central toolchain inevitably becomes the bottleneck for the few teams that legitimately need to move faster or operate outside the standard mold. 

It creates a tension between platform stability and edge-case velocity.

Now, when you flip the switch to a "build it yourself," componentized toolchain, providing pipeline examples as a North Star but no hard limitations on what a pipeline should do, you get a broad spectrum of outcomes. 

You’ll see some teams investing incredible time into elaborate, bespoke toolchains serving their very specific (and often self-perceived) needs. 

Meanwhile, others remain in the Continuous Delivery Stone Age, with hardly any build or deployment automation to speak of.

I have landed on the belief that the centralized approach works very well in small to midsized companies. 

However, sheer organizational entropy forces the decentralized approach at most big companies. 

This is a shame, because it leaves these large enterprises without the critical levers to enforce a continuous flow of functional, secure, and compliant software. 

When every team invents their own deployment wheel, you don't just lose efficiency, you lose the ability to measure system-wide health and, more critically, to patch a zero-day vulnerability across the estate in a matter of hours.

What I'm observing now is the emergence of a necessary third path: 

Opinionated Platforms with Paved Road Escape Hatches.

We can't pretend that a single YAML schema will satisfy both the front-end SPA team and the legacy mainframe integration team. 

The future of this isn't "choose one or the other." 

It's about providing a golden path that handles 90% of use cases (security scanning, attestation, artifact promotion) but allows the 10% of complex teams to bring their own execution environment, provided they conform to a standardized contract for observability and artifact signing.

The goal shouldn't be to make every pipeline identical. 

The goal should be to make every pipeline verifiable.

#DevOps #PlatformEngineering #CICD #SoftwareDelivery #TechStrategy #DeveloperExperience

posts.text:
If you’ve ever been in a team with internal customers, such as a platform engineering or infrastructure team, you probably know more than well the flip side of having an open Teams or Slack channel where customers can chat with you about their problems.

While the ambition is honourable, the effects are far from it.

These channels quickly become the go-to places for customers with random thoughts, issues, problems, and everything in between. 

Why bother looking information up on a wiki when it’s much easier to fire off a question? 

Sometimes without sufficient context at the start, requiring the chat conversation to drag on while the clock ticks.

There are also no statistical tools in chat services that can collect:

- The number of interactions.
- The types of interactions.
- The number of resolutions.
- The time from initiation to resolution.

So to summarise: while using chat as a support channel for internal customers is honourable, it comes with its drawbacks.

Switching over to only responding to customer requests through tickets is neither optimal. 

Tickets are often impersonal, requiring a lot of context to be provided for resolution, and overall fairly inefficient. 

On the other hand, the statistics are easily collectible.

Is now the time we’ll see a continuation of the chat as a support channel, but with AI bots taking over first-line inquiries while being trained on the team’s knowledge base articles, internal documentation, run- and playbooks?

Has anyone witnessed / used a bot, that goes beyond regular key word and pattern recognition and actually provides working solutions to customer issues? 

#PlatformEngineering #DevOps #ChatOps #DeveloperExperience #GenerativeAI #TechLeadership

posts.text:
Back in 2018, in a previous company, as we were starting our GCP journey, I had a specific vision for how we should structure the cloud.

I wanted total micro-segmentation. 

The idea was: Give every feature team their own GCP projects. Complete isolation. Complete ownership. 

Platform engineering teams would be granted sufficient but not excessive IAM permissions to manage the infrastructure (K8s, DBs, networking etc), but the overall cost, security, and operational accountability would be primarily with the feature team.

The goal was to merge operational excellence with true "You Build It, You Run It" autonomy. 

I argued that this visibility would make FinOps, operational and security issues immediate and self-evident. 

No more "who owns this idle IP address?" It would be obvious.

We didn't go that way.

The counter-arguments were valid at the time: Management overhead, cost sprawl, and a lack of automation maturity to govern hundreds of isolated estates. 

We landed on a more familiar, on-prem style setup: a single, large GKE cluster and SQL on GCE. 

(Reality eventually forced GKE cluster segmentation anyway.)

When I joined another company, a couple of years later, I was thrilled to see that the micro-segmentation vision wasn't just possible; it was implemented. 

But seeing it live taught me the lessons we hadn't learned in 2018. Here are three that I carry with me now:

1. The "Readiness" Trap is Self-Fulfilling
We said the teams weren’t ready to automate infrastructure management across hundreds of projects. In hindsight, that was backwards. You don't build automation muscle by managing one big cluster. You build it by being forced to manage ten or fifty. The pain of scale is the catalyst for writing the Terraform modules and GitOps pipelines.

2. The Hidden Cost of "Soft" Multi-Tenancy
Running one big cluster looked cheaper on the cloud bill. But what we saved in GCP project overhead, we paid for tenfold in human coordination overhead. Every cluster upgrade, every new CRD installation, every admission controller tweak became a cross-team negotiation with a massive blast radius. Infrastructure cost is linear and predictable; coordination cost is exponential and hidden.

3. The Network Becomes the Product
When running segmentation at scale, the biggest challenge aren’t the VMs or the pods. It is the spaces between the isolated projects. That’s why you must treat connectivity as a declarative API. Feature teams shouldn't request firewall rules; they should declare: "Service A needs to talk to Service B." The platform then uses automation to create that specific, narrow pipe and lifecycle it.

Micro-segmentation is the superior long-term model for velocity and accountability. 

But it only works if you abstract away the toil of the spaces between. 

#PlatformEngineering #GCP #DevOps #CloudStrategy #FinOps #GKE #Microsegmentation

posts.text:
The classic techie origin story goes something like this: ”I was 6 or 7 years old when I unscrewed my first computer to figure out how everything worked, and I’ve kept at it ever since.”

I’ve heard everyone from tech company founders to grad students repeating it. 

It’s become so embedded in the tech space way of thinking that no one questions it, we all just nod in recognition and silent agreement.

However, I deeply suspect that most of us didn’t unscrew our computers, to understand them from the inside out. 

That’s why it was so incredibly refreshing recently to hear a junior engineer shrug it off. 

As we were discussing the implications of AI on his job as a software engineer, he said something that cut through the noise: 

”I love my job, but it’s not my sole purpose in life. If I wasn't coding, I'd be doing something physical, probably carpentry."

No panic. No existential crisis about the industry shifting. Just a grounded sense of self.

I can so relate to this point of view. 

I’ve been close to computers and technology since basically elementary school, started my own web development shop in high school, and went on to study engineering physics. But I can’t say that computers and technology have been the sole thing on my mind.

That breadth of perspective, the ability to see engineering as a discipline rather than an identity, is a superpower we don't talk about enough. 

It’s what allows us to adapt to AI tools instead of fearing them.

Having interests beyond the terminal doesn't dilute your technical edge; it makes you more versatile and more resilient. 

Whether it's wood grain or code, you're just building things.

#SoftwareEngineering #AI #CareerResilience #TechCulture #EngineeringMindset

posts.text:
I just finished Gregor Hohpe’s “Platform Strategy” while on vacation in Malta, the last pages consumed just before touchdown in Stockholm. 🇲🇹✈️🇸🇪

Having worked with, and more importantly, seen the anti-types of, platform engineering for almost a decade, I’m glad to have internalised and embraced many of the book’s findings and recommendations into my own ways of working.

Gregor rightfully points out that platform engineering is a major cognitive shift from traditional IT infrastructure and operations. 

It is not "Infrastructure as Code with a nicer UI." 

It is not "DevOps with a portal." 

Companies that fail to recognise this simple premise aren't just moving slowly; they are pouring money and effort into a failed endeavour.

The hardest part is the mind shift from "The Department of No" to "The Team of Now".

Having personally seen numerous times how difficult it is for operations teams to shift from limit and control to enable and monitor, the book comes with some welcome pointers for those brave enough to venture on this journey.

The mind shift is also about replacing the project management centricity with a product management mindset, something that’s neither natural nor easy in traditional IT. 

Here are three additional context points from own trenches that echo Gregor’s guidance, which might help anyone currently navigating this shift:

- Thinnest Viable Platform (TVP) over "The Big Build": You cannot boil the ocean. The most successful platform initiatives I've seen started by solving one specific, painful cognitive load for a single stream-aligned team. The platform is never done; it's a living product.

- Throttling is a Smell: When you see a platform team acting as a gatekeeper for every CI/CD pipeline change or every new AWS S3 bucket, you are not looking at a platform. You are looking at a bottleneck with a fancy new title. The goal of the platform is to make the "golden path" so attractive that developers want to stay on it, but the gravel path is still visible so they can run ahead when they need to.

- You Can't Automate a Bad Process: This is an old BPR adage, but it’s the iceberg that sinks Platform Engineering initiatives. If the underlying process requires 4 approval tickets in ServiceNow, putting a React UI over that API doesn't make it a Platform, it makes it a gilded cage. You have to fix the decision rights first.

As with Gregor’s other books, the language is rich yet accessible, full of synonyms and (car) metaphors that help underline the valuable messages without ever feeling like vendor fluff.

This is the go-to book if you’re interested in platform engineering and what benefits it can unlock in your organisation. 

It’s not a marketing prop but a useful manual that can help you steer off many treacherous paths in your platform engineering adoption journey.

#PlatformEngineering #GregorHohpe #DevOps #CloudStrategy #TechLeadership #Malta #BookReview

posts.text:
I'm currently reading Gregor Hohpe's ”Platform Strategy” book, and one concept stopped me cold: the Grim Wrapper.

Most useful abstractions are happy wrappers – they simplify, shield complexity, and make life better. 

But then there are the grim ones. 

You know them by their symptoms:

❌ They can't keep up with the underlying platforms’ rate of change (hello, chasing every CSP (Cloud Service Provider) release)

❌ Their "simplicity" is delusional – error conditions surface from components users never knew existed, spiking cognitive load

❌ External knowledge becomes useless – every internal user must be taught from scratch

❌ You're building for the lowest common denominator, leaving power users frustrated

❌ They add massive operational burden for vanishingly little benefit

This hit home. 

I once sat through discussions where some participants advocated wrapping hundreds of Cloud Service Provider APIs behind an internal API gateway. 

The rationale? "Consistency" and "abstraction” to name two arguments.

The reality? 

A potentially tremendous effort, prone to every pitfall above, with almost no benefit to end users.

Luckily, reason prevailed.

Before you wrap that cloud service or open source project, ask: Are we building a happy wrapper – or a grim one?

#PlatformStrategy #SoftwareArchitecture #GregorHohpe #CloudComputing #TechLeadership

posts.text:
I just finished ”We” by Yevgeny Zamyatin, continuing my streak of dystopian fiction, and I remain in awe of how a novel written in 1921 still feels so startlingly fresh.

Zamyatin’s vision is precise and unsettling. 

His story is set in a future human society following some apocalyptic collapse of world order. 

Men live behind a gigantic wall, confined to streets and buildings made out of glass so that the One State may have constant access to its subjects. 

Humans no longer go by names but numbers, and all have a distinct purpose in life: writers, mathematicians, poets and so on. 

Happiness can only be achieved by giving up freedom, so in order for the numbers, or Unifs, short for uniform, to be happy, they must relinquish their personal freedoms of thought, action, and even imagination.

At the center of the book is the love story between two numbers. 

D-503 is a mathematician dedicated to reason and order, initially totally devoted to the One State and its “final” revolution. 

I-330 is fierce and unafraid to question the norm. 

She sways him over, and he eventually joins and helps the resistance movement that she is a part of. 

It is through her that D-503 begins to understand what his mathematical world has excluded: the irrational, the messy, the human. 

As he falls in love, he reflects: “It was then that I felt clearly for the first time that a number can be in love, but only a number that is a man, not a number that is only a digit in a phone directory.”

Zamyatin gives us a narrator torn between two worlds: the crystalline, transparent order of the state and the dangerous, chaotic pulse of individual feeling. 

D-503 initially scorns imagination, writing with pride: “We have no reason to envy the wild poets of the past. Their flights of fancy merely prove how primitive their ‘state of mind’ was.” 

Yet as the novel progresses, he discovers that imagination is not a flaw but the very thing that makes us free, and the state knows it.

The ending is devastating. Towards the end, after the One State starts being onto the resistance movement, and after he meets the Benefactor, D-503 undergoes a lobotomy to suppress all imagination, including emotions. 

The final pages, clinical and hollow, show a man who has been cured of his humanity. 

It is a conclusion that forces you to ask: is a world without inner conflict worth having?

What makes We so remarkable is not just its influence, it directly shaped Brave New World and 1984, but its ferocious intelligence. 

Zamyatin wrote not only against political tyranny but against the very idea that human beings can be reduced to formulas. 

Decades later, the novel remains a haunting exploration of love, reason, and the cost of conformity.

If you care about literature that challenges, disturbs, and endures, We belongs on your shelf.

#BookReview #WeByZamyatin #DystopianFiction #Literature #Soviet #Taylorism #ManvsMachine #DeusExMachina

Ahead: a week off on Easter leave, then a new adventure in a really exciting place.

I’ve been asked if there are any decisions or commitments in my mailbox that need to be stored or shared before I go.

I’ve thought it through. Honestly? There aren’t any.

Not because nothing’s been going on, far from it, but because of how I’ve tried to lead.

My leadership style has always been about involving the people affected early in decision-making. 

That means by the time a decision is made, the ones who need to act on it already have the context, the information, and the mandate to move forward.

What leaves with me is my own perspective, experiences, and knowledge, but not the context. 

That’s already with the team, where it belongs.

Inclusive leadership, to me, means building so that no one becomes a bottleneck. 

Not even yourself.

Grateful for the journey. Ready for what’s next.

#NewBeginnings #Leadership #SEB #KnowledgeSharing #InclusiveLeadership

posts.text:
Probably the worst combo in enterprise is when individual mandate and accountability is unclear and when decisions are made far from where they are executed.

This is the perfect storm where structure, authority, and accountability collide.

When individual mandate is unclear, no single person feels the weight of success or failure. This creates:

- The “Bystander Effect” in business: Everyone assumes someone else is responsible. Critical tasks fall through the cracks.

- Risk aversion: Why take a bold risk if you don’t have clear ownership of the outcome? Instead, people focus on “covering themselves” with emails and approvals.

- The inability to learn: When something fails, there’s no single point of failure to analyze. Post-mortems turn into blame-avoidance exercises rather than learning opportunities.

When decisions are made far from where they are executed, you lose:

- Context: Leaders in upper management lack the on-the-ground information (customer nuances, technical debt, local market conditions) to make the right call.

- Speed: Every decision requires a pilgrimage up the chain of command. By the time approval comes down, the opportunity has passed or the crisis has worsened.

- Ownership: People on the front lines stop feeling like “owners” and start feeling like “executors.” They disengage. If a decision made remotely fails, those executing it say, “I just did what I was told,” while the remote decision-maker says, “They didn’t execute it properly.”

These two elements feed off each other:

- Because accountability is unclear, leaders hesitate to push decision-making authority down. They fear that if they delegate, chaos will ensue since no one is “in charge.”

- Because decisions are made remotely, accountability becomes even more diluted. The people doing the work can’t be held accountable for outcomes they didn’t control, and the people making the calls can’t be held accountable for contexts they didn’t understand.

If you are in an organization suffering from this combo, you will see:

- Escalation inertia: Everything gets escalated to “leadership,” creating a bottleneck.

- Performative work: Teams focus on creating slides for leadership approvals rather than delivering value to customers.

- High attrition: Competent people leave because they have the responsibility of execution but neither the authority to decide nor the clarity on whether they are actually accountable for the result.

The inverse of this is the formula for high-performance organizations:

1. Push decision-making to the information, not information to the decision-makers.

2. Make accountability singular. If two people are accountable, no one is accountable.

3. Tolerate local optimization. Empowered teams making contextually correct decisions (even if they aren’t globally “perfect”) will always outperform centralized command-and-control structures over time.

#Accountability #Mandate #Ownership #DecisionMaking #Leadership #LearnedHelplessness #Bureaucracy

posts.text:
This week, students from my daughter’s school, including her, took part in the yearly visit to Auschwitz and Birkenau.

It is a heavy tradition, but an invaluable one.

A few students are selected to go on this important journey, and in turn, they are obliged to share their impressions, reflections, and thoughts with the rest of the school.

It is a tradition that is deeply appreciated by all the students, even as it invokes powerful feelings and difficult thoughts among the teenagers.

I wanted to reshare a short caption from their reflections. Their words speak for themselves:


"Yesterday we were in Auschwitz, where we saw things you can hardly imagine. When we saw everything with our own eyes, it became something completely different. Everything became so real. It is important that we come here so we do not forget history, because otherwise, it will repeat itself. It is also important so that we can tell, in our own words, what we saw to those who have not been there. We believe that if you have the opportunity to go to Auschwitz, you should."


In a world where authoritarian winds are blowing harder across the globe, these words are more urgent than ever.

It is not enough to simply remember; we must act against hatred, intolerance and bigotry.

It is our shared responsibility to protect the weak, the unshielded, and the vulnerable.

There is no more important investment than teaching the next generation to bear witness, speak up, and recognize the warning signs of hatred, wherever it may be aimed.

#EducationMatters #HumanRights #Democracy #Auschwitz #Remembrance #Holocaust

posts.text:
Yesterday, I had a fascinating conversation with a friend. He was convinced he had the best manager in the world, because this manager constantly shielded the team, acted as a buffer against leadership, and never forced the team to adhere to what he framed as "BS company policies."

I challenged him on that perspective. I explained that while this manager excels at the first phase of management, he is inadvertently doing himself and the team a great disservice.

In my experience, effective management evolves through three distinct phases:

Phase 1: Safety & Trust
This is the foundational layer. It’s about psychological safety, creating security within the team, and building trust between team members and toward the manager. Without this, nothing else works. It’s where my friend’s manager is currently stuck.

Phase 2: Clarity & Structure
This is where management matures. It involves understanding the expectations outside the team, the organizational rules, processes, and strategic constraints that the team inevitably must adhere to. A great leader doesn’t just shield the team from these; they translate them. They help the team navigate reality while identifying opportunities for improvement within the system, the team’s workflow, and individual capabilities.

Phase 3: Balance & Delivery
This is the ultimate stage. It’s the art of striking a sound balance between organizational demands and team well-being. It focuses on making progress on deliveries, refining the team’s operating model, and aligning individual development with professional fulfillment.

Managers who stay too long in "Phase 1" often operate under the guise of being a "hero." They absorb pressure rather than teaching the team to manage it. They hide context rather than empowering the team to make informed decisions.

But here is the reality: when a manager refuses to enforce necessary processes or engage with the wider organizational context, they aren't being kind; they are creating a bubble.

Eventually, this approach leads to disappointment for all parties:

- Team members become fragile, unable to navigate corporate realities or advocate for themselves. They get blindsided when leadership changes or reorgs happen.

- Management and stakeholders lose trust, viewing the team as an outlier, a "black box," or a bottleneck that refuses to align.

- The manager ends up burned out, acting as a human shield indefinitely, often leaving under the weight of their own unsustainable creation.

The best managers don’t just build safe havens; they build bridges. 

They use the trust from Phase 1 to have the hard conversations in Phase 2, so they can achieve sustainable success in Phase 3.

My friend has a nice manager. 

But a truly great manager is one who prepares you to thrive, not just within the team, but within the entire organization.

#Management #Leadership #CareerAdvice #PsychologicalSafety #TeamDevelopment #LeadershipPhases

posts.text:
“When Gregor Samsa woke one morning from uneasy dreams, he found himself transformed in his bed into a monstrous vermin.”

I remember almost as if it was yesterday the first time I read the first few pages of Kafka’s The Metamorphosis in high school. 

It was for a literature class, and the excerpt was chosen to represent Kafka’s distinct style and topicality.

I’ve read other Kafka books since then, but it’s only now that I finally finished ”The Metamorphosis” in full.

It’s a fascinating short novel: travelling salesman Gregor Samsa wakes up one morning in his room, lying in bed as a large insect. 

His situation becomes a major setback, not only for himself, but for his elderly parents and younger sister, all of whom rely on him as the family’s breadwinner. 

Over time, as he sinks deeper into depression and resignation, Gregor finds that the family’s life moves on, profoundly affected, yet strangely able to continue without him.

At the end, as Gregor dies from starvation (by his own choosing), the rest of the family finds comfort in finally being free from their “most peculiar circumstances”: sharing their roof with a large insect that used to be a family member.

What makes the book extraordinary is how its intent and message shift depending on the reader, exactly as Kafka intended. 

He famously instructed his first book illustrator not to depict the insect, wanting each reader to envision the transformation in their own way.

“The insect itself cannot be drawn. It cannot be shown even from a distance.”

To me, Gregor’s metamorphosis reads as an analogy for a family member who goes down the wrong path, or becomes otherwise unrecognizable. 

Initially, the parents and sister are deeply concerned and engaged, wishing the nightmare would end. 

But gradually, disappointment and disillusion set in, and they begin to give up on him.

It’s a classical masterpiece, and well worth the short read. 

Sometimes the most powerful stories are the ones that let us bring our own interpretation to the page.

#FranzKafka #TheMetamorphosis #DieVerwandlung #BookReview

Last night was a birthday gift to remember. 

My wife and I attended Bo Kaspers Orkester’s 35-year jubilee at Göta Lejon, and it was more than just a nostalgia trip.

They delivered all the classics, from “Undantag” to “Vi kommer aldrig att dö”, but what struck me most was their skill to actualize songs written long ago to today’s realities.

The highlight was a powerful twist on “Dansa på min grav.”

Paired with visuals of marching North Korean, Chinese, American troops, images of world autocrats, and their lackeys, the lyrics hit with maximum impact:

”De vill lära dig att krypa, att gå i takt
De vill lära dig marschera, att stå givakt
Stå på rad och klappa händer, för sakens bästa hålla med
Lära dig hur vinden vänder, för sakens bästa gå på led

De vill lära dig att ljuga när det tar emot
Lära dig bocka och att buga för någon idiot
Jag vill lära dig att dansa om det någon gång blir av
Om jag får nått att säga till om, då skall du dansa på min grav

De vill lära dig att tvivla på det du tror
Gå omkring och vara rädd för din syster och din bror
Jag vill lära dig att dansa om det någon gång blir av
Om jag får nått att säga till om, då skall du dansa på min grav”

It was incredibly well paired; recognition and provocation wrapped in a world-class performance.

And the real takeaway? Great art ages like a fine wine. 

Hearing “Landet vi ärvde” or “Semester” framed by current events reminded me that the best bands don’t just play their hits, they make you hear them for the first time again.

Throw in an amazing lighting and visual show, and you have a band that isn’t just celebrating a legacy; they’re proving they are still essential.

35 years in, and they’re still making us think. 

Thank you for an evening #IlMagnifico!

#BoKaspersOrkester #GötaLejon #LiveMusic #TimelessArt #Music #ConcertReview #Stockholm

posts.text:
I learnt a new term today at the #MicrosoftAITour. 

Forget about companies born in the cloud. 

The next aspiration level is ”frontier firm”. 

The term describes the destination, not the starting point. 

Think of it less as a strict category and more as a spectrum.

On one end are the companies still in the "PoC phase," experimenting with a single agent or a copilot for internal use. This is the vast majority.

On the other end are the true "frontier firms." These are the organizations that have successfully navigated the chasm from PoC to production at scale. 

For them, AI agents aren't a side project; they are the core infrastructure for customer engagement, underwriting, supply chains, etc.

So, how many companies would classify as frontier firms? 

Right now, very, very few. 

Why are then most companies ”stuck” in the PoC phase with agents?

1. The "Cool Demo" vs. The Boring Backend: 

It's easy to build a demo agent that can answer questions from a PDF. It's incredibly hard to build an agent that can securely access your core transaction systems, understand complex customer context, and take action without making a costly error. The "front end" of the AI talk is the agent; the "back end" is #cloudnative infrastructure, data integration, governance, and security.

2. The "Shadow AI" Problem: 

If the IT side is slow to move from PoC to production, business side will go around it. They'll use consumer-grade AI tools to get their jobs done, creating massive data leaks and security vulnerabilities. This makes the IT side even more cautious, slowing the whole process down.

3. Process Redesign is Hard: 

A true "frontier firm" doesn't just automate an old process with an agent. It reimagines the process entirely. That requires significant organizational change management, upskilling, and often a cultural shift that's much harder than the technology itself.

What Separates a Frontier Firm from the Rest?

It's not size or industry. 

It's about crossing that chasm. The firms that will truly classify are the ones that:

- Move from "Ask" to "Do": They graduate from agents that simply answer questions (a chatbot 2.0) to agents that can execute transactions, update records, and orchestrate workflows across multiple systems.

- Solve the Governance Paradox: They find the sweet spot between enabling innovation (allowing teams to experiment) and enforcing policies and security. They build a "guardrails" approach, not a "gates" approach.

- Think in Systems, Not Snippets: They don't see agents as isolated tools. They see a future ecosystem of agents working together; a customer-facing agent, a logistics agent, a fraud-detection agent, all collaborating.

The companies that treat this as just another tech cycle will be left behind. 

The ones that treat it as a fundamental re-architecting of the firm, they will be the ones on the frontier.

#AI #agents #agentic #cloud #infrastructure #governance #security #Azure #GCP #AWS #Nvidia #OpenClaw #NemoClaw

posts.text:
The colour historically associated with the patron saint of Ireland wasn't green; it was blue ("St. Patrick's Blue").

But during the Irish Rebellion, the English red coat was opposed by the Irish green. 

The shamrock ☘️ became the symbol of national pride.

Green became the colour of Ireland because it was the colour of rebellion. 

It represented the fight for independence, the landscape, and the hope of a nation.

After the rebellion was crushed, the English authorities moved to suppress all symbols of the defeated uprising. 

Wearing green became an act of sedition, and people could be severely punished for it. 

This period of repression is lamented in the Irish ballad "The Wearing of the Green," with its heartbreaking line about hanging men and women for simply wearing the colour.

So, in short, green was a unifying standard for a new Irish identity, and a symbol so powerful that wearing it could cost you your life.

Today, it’s a celebration of identity. 

From a political statement to a global party, that’s the power of a colour. ☘️

#StPatricksDay #green #Eire #Ireland

posts.text:
A few years ago, I was having a discussion with some friends about democracies around the world and how surprising it was that some authoritarian countries still hadn’t embraced democracy.

Their responses startled me somewhat. 

They themselves had backgrounds in countries with authoritarian regimes and, although these were not the same countries, their arguments echoed each other. 

It basically went along the lines that not all countries and cultures were suited for democracy and that democracy was a Western invention that didn’t have a natural place in their homelands.

These were otherwise very logical and intelligent people, so their arguments surprised me. 

For me, the human rights such as the right to life, liberty, freedom from torture, right to a fair trial, freedom of expression, and the right to work and education as they are worded in the The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), adopted by the UN in 1948, are rock solid. But here were people I knew well suggesting that none of it was a given.

I have just finished Anne Applebaum's “Autocracy, Inc.”, and I have had a profound realization: the arguments my friends repeated so earnestly were not original observations. 

They were echoes, talking points propagated by state-owned media from these autocracies.

Applebaum argues persuasively that we are not simply dealing with a clash of ideologies (democracy vs. communism) anymore. 

Instead, we are witnessing the rise of a global network held together by kleptocracy rather than ideology, and by a shared hunger for power rather than moral conviction. 

Autocrats across the world are no longer ideological allies; they are business partners propping each other up, sharing technology for surveillance and repression, and laundering money through the same opaque financial systems.

What makes the book so compelling is the sheer volume of examples and personal accounts that illustrate how these networks operate; from Russia, Hungary, Turkey to Iran, from China, North Korea, Myanmar to Venezuela, Cuba and to other corners of the world. It is a stark reminder that the erosion of democracy is a coordinated, transnational business.

Applebaum finishes the book with a personal encouragement for all democratically minded people across the world: fight autocracies everywhere! 

Her message is clear; this is not a battle that can be won by staying within our own borders.

My conversation from a few years ago taught me that the first line of defense for democracy is recognizing that these "cultural" arguments against freedom are manufactured. 

The second is realizing that we are all in this together.

#AutocracyInc #AnneApplebaum #Democracy #HumanRights #UDHR #GlobalAffairs #ReadingList

posts.text:
The fastest way to create cloud chaos? Allow every cloud service by default.

Most teams focus on landing zones, security, IAM, networking…

But here’s the uncomfortable truth:

👉 If you don’t govern which cloud services people can use, the rest of your foundation doesn’t matter.

Why? Because an unguided service catalog is where chaos begins.

If you're defining a Landing Zone, here’s why your service catalog needs strong, unapologetic governance:

1️⃣ Regional Availability & Compliance
Not every cloud service supports your required regions or local endpoints.
If data can’t stay local → it can’t be used.
Simple as that.

2️⃣ Contractual & Commercial Soundness
A service may not be covered by your enterprise agreements.
If you enable it without licensing validation, you’re taking a financial and legal gamble you don’t need to take.

3️⃣ Framework Alignment (Security & Compliance)
Does it meet FedRAMP, NIST, CIS, or your own internal instructions/frameworks?
If not, it doesn’t belong in production, especially not for sensitive workloads.

4️⃣ Policy Enforcement
Can you actually enforce your guardrails?
If the answer is “not really,” the service probably belongs in permanent preview purgatory.

5️⃣ Architectural Fit & Standardization
Just because the service exists doesn’t mean it belongs in your ecosystem.
Developer freedom matters, but not at the cost of creating an operational Frankenstein.

In the cloud, saying “yes” is easy.

Saying “yes” responsibly requires a strong, curated, well‑governed service catalog.

Your cloud foundation depends on it.

#CloudComputing #CloudFoundation #CloudGovernance #ServiceCatalog #FinOps #TechStrategy #GCP #AWS #Azure #Compliance #FinancialServices

posts.text:
I sometimes meet a misconception that to do Disaster Recovery (DR) in the cloud, you must have a multi-region, active-active setup.

Sure, if you are Netflix, or if your application requires state-of-the-art resilience (like global financial trading), that might be the benchmark. 

But for the other 99% of us? That approach is an overkill and a budget-breaker.

Disaster recovery can be achieved much cheaper and more efficiently than spinning up duplicate infrastructure everywhere. 

It starts with two critical business metrics: RPO and RTO.

1. RPO (Recovery Point Objective): This is your tolerance for data loss.

- Example: If your RPO is 6 hours, you can afford to lose at most 6 hours of data. Your backup strategy (snapshots, database logs) must be designed to meet that window.

- Context: For many workloads, backing data across multiple Availability Zones (AZs) within a single region is sufficient to survive server failures. However, recent geopolitical events and high-profile regional outages have taught us that "regional isolation" can be a real risk. 

A wise, and relatively cheap, compromise is to copy your immutable backups to an alternative region asynchronously. 

This covers a regional disaster without the complexity of live traffic there.

2. RTO (Recovery Time Objective): This is the clock ticking until you must be back online.

- Having a fully active-active setup across regions would give you the lowest RTO (seconds/minutes), but developing that architecture and paying for the "hot" duplicate infrastructure is expensive.

- The Sweet Spot: Focus on resilience within a single region first (multi-AZ setups handle the vast majority of outages). Then, plan for the worst-case scenario (region down) with a warm standby or pilot light approach.

The Key to True DR (Without the Netflix Budget):

If an entire region goes dark, you don't need live traffic there 24/7. You need to be able to restore elsewhere quickly.

- Infrastructure as Code (IaC): If your entire environment is defined in code, you can spin up your entire stack in a new region in minutes, not days.

- Data Portability: Ensure your backups or replicated data are accessible in that DR region.

- Automation: Develop a runbook and practice a "failover" exercise. Knowing your RTO is 4 hours means you need to prove you can restore a working application from your cross-region backups within that time frame.

The takeaway?

You don't need to live everywhere to survive everywhere. 

Design for zone resilience, backup for region failure, and automate your recovery.

#CloudComputing #DisasterRecovery #DevOps #GCP #AWS #Azure #Architecture #regional #zonal #multiregional #backup

posts.text:
That moment the Wi-Fi drops and you feel the world go silent.

You know the drill. The frantic toggling of the router. The mute prayer that it’s just a temporary glitch, not a sign that your hardware, or your sanity, is about to give out.

Now, imagine if your entire existence depended on a single machine. And one day, it just… stopped.

That’s the premise of E.M. Forster’s eerily prophetic 1909 novella, The Machine Stops. 

I recently read it, and it’s staggering how this story, written over a century ago, reads like a manual for our modern digital anxieties.

In Forster’s world, humanity lives underground in isolated chambers. 

The Machine caters to their every need: food, climate, information. 

Physical interaction is a rarity, replaced by instant, constant virtual communication. 

Travel happens, but it’s uncomfortable and uncommon. Why risk the surface when everything you could possibly want is delivered through your walls?

Forster never explicitly states why everyone is underground, but the implication is clear: the surface became uninhabitable, likely due to some ecological or societal collapse. 

The Machine is no longer just a convenience; it is a life-support system.

Sound familiar?

We’re not living in underground cells (yet), but how many of us feel a tremor of panic when the connection drops? 

We work remotely, socialize via screens, and trust algorithms to feed us, inform us, and connect us. We’ve built our own Machine, and we’re wholly dependent on it.

The novel isn’t just a critique of technology; it’s a profound exploration of:

- The fragility of complex systems: What happens when the infrastructure we take for granted fails?

- The illusion of connection: Are we truly connecting, or just communicating in a vacuum?

- The atrophy of lived experience: When the Machine provides everything, what do we lose? The ability to tolerate discomfort. The skill of face-to-face interaction. The resilience born from navigating the unmediated world.

Forster’s vision is a powerful reminder. As we race to build the next AI, the next metaverse, or the next layer of convenience, we have to ask ourselves: 

Are we building a utopia, or are we just making ourselves comfortable in a cage?

Have we traded the unpredictable, messy, beautiful surface for the predictable hum of the Machine? And if it ever stopped, would we even remember how to live without it?

P.S. Big thanks to Erik Schön for the book recommendation!

posts.text:
Individual accountability is a function of organizational design.

Over the years, I have observed a simple equation that holds true more often than not:

Accountability = f( 1/Size x 1/Proximity )

Proportion of individual accountability at work is a function of inverse size of the company and inverse proximity to end customer. 

The larger the company and the further away from the end customer, the lower the individual accountability.

This dynamic can be explained by two powerful psychological and organizational forces: the Ringelmann Effect and the concept of Line of Sight.

The Ringelmann Effect explains the "inverse size" function. 

Identified in early 20th-century experiments, it demonstrates that as group size increases, individual effort decreases due to a diffusion of responsibility. 

Ringelmann identified two causes: 

Coordination loss (people getting in each other's way) and, more importantly, Motivation loss (what we now call social loafing).

In a large company, an individual feels their contribution is a negligible drop in the ocean. 

If the company succeeds or fails, the individual's personal "pull on the rope" is imperceptible, removing the psychological incentive to exert maximum effort. 

This is the pure math of social loafing: the larger the crowd, the easier it is to hide.

Compounding this is the lack of Line of Sight, which defines the "inverse proximity" function. 

Line of sight is the psychological connection between one's daily actions and the ultimate outcome for the end user. 

When an employee is far removed from the customer, buried under layers of management, process, or bureaucracy, the consequence of their work (or lack thereof) becomes abstract. 

There is no visceral feedback loop. 

Without seeing the customer struggle or succeed because of their effort, the work becomes about fulfilling internal metrics rather than solving real human problems. The employee is blind to the damage caused by their disengagement.

These two forces create a destructive feedback loop. 

The Ringelmann Effect makes the individual feel their effort doesn't matter, while a lack of line of sight makes them blind to the consequences. 

The result is the lowest possible state of individual accountability. 

Conversely, shrinking the effective unit size counteracts Ringelmann, and forcibly creating proximity to the customer restores the line of sight, rebuilding the psychological foundation for ownership and responsibility.

How do we fix this?

- Shrink the effective unit: Empower "two-pizza teams" to operate with clear mandate, accountability and ownership.

- Force proximity: Engineers need to be on support rotation. Product managers should take every chance to observe and engage with users. Build the bridge to your customers.

#Leadership #OrganizationalBehavior #CompanyCulture #Accountability #Ownership #Mandate #Management #OrganisationalDesign #Ringelmann #LineOfSight #SocialLoafing #CustomerCentricity

posts.text:
I just finished listening to Matt Abrahams’ Think Faster, Talk Smarter, and I have mixed, but mostly positive, thoughts.

It is a genuinely good book packed with tactical advice for anyone who has ever frozen during a Q&A, stumbled through a check-in, or felt put on the spot. 

However, if you are familiar with Abrahams’ work (as his Think Fast, Talk Smart podcast), this book feels very much like a written extension of that format. 

So, if you can get past the "podcasty" feel, the tips and tricks are absolute gold. 

Here are the key takeaways I’m implementing immediately:

1. Manage the "Amygdala Hijack" with Physical Tricks 

That panic feeling is real. Abrahams provides a great table of techniques for "Minding Your Anxiety Symptom ABCs." The trick isn't to eliminate anxiety, but to manage it physically:

- Breathe (The 2:1 Rule): Inhale deeply, filling your lower abdomen, and exhale twice as long as you inhale.

- Cool Your Body: Hold a cool bottle of water in your hands to reduce blushing and perspiration.

- Salivate: Chew gum or enjoy a pastille to reactivate dry salivary glands caused by stress.

2. Get Out of Your Own Way 

We are our own worst critics. Abrahams suggests some mindset shifts to break free:

- Dare to Be Dull: Stop focusing on "doing it right." When you stop monitoring your performance for perfection, you actually become more creative and free.

- Embrace Mistakes: Reframe failure as a "missed take" in a movie. It’s just part of the process, not the final product.

- Dialogue, Not Monologue: Remember that you aren't a "sage on a stage." Interact and ask questions to make it a two-way street.

3. Structure Spontaneity (The Frameworks) 

Abrahams argues that the most confident speakers rely on simple, go-to structures. The book provides "Classic All-Purpose Structures" and specific ones for high-stakes moments. Here are a few I’m keeping in my back pocket:

- What > So What > Now What: Perfect for small talk or giving an update. Discuss the topic (What), why it matters (So What), and what’s next (Now What).

- Problem > Solution > Benefit: Ideal for pitching. Evoke an issue, offer a solution, and end by discussing the benefit.

- The ADD Structure for Q&A: Answer the question in a single sentence, Detail an example that supports your answer, and Describe the benefits relevant to the asker.

- The AAA for Apologies: Acknowledge the offending behavior, Appreciate how it impacted others, and Act by detailing how you will make amends.

-The 4 I's for Feedback: Provide Information, explain the Impact, extend an Invitation to discuss, and detail the Implications.

4. The Power of the Pause 

We think silences are awkward, but the audience perceives pauses as thoughtfulness. Taking a breath before answering makes you look smarter, not slower.

#ThinkFastTalkSmart #Communication #PublicSpeaking #BookReview #ProfessionalDevelopment #MattAbrahams #CommunicationSkills #SpontaneousSpeaking

posts.text:
It’s funny how the simplest phrases often carry the most weight.

20 years ago, I was working in B2B sales at Vodafone. I was surrounded by sales targets, commission structures, and the high-pressure energy of chasing deals. 

During that time, one of the Key Account Managers I worked with, said something in passing that has quite literally lived in my skull ever since:

"Only when you have stability and support at home, can you have success at work."

Those words stuck. Not because they were revolutionary or complex, but because in their simplicity, they uncovered the core truth about the relationship between life and work.

In the corporate world, we often treat "work-life balance" as a buzzword, something we put on slides but rarely examine honestly. 

We look at high performers and study their habits, their routines, their tactics. But we often ignore the foundation they are standing on.

If your personal life is chaotic, if your relationships are strained, if you are mentally exhausted from dealing with instability outside of office hours, the "success" you find at work is usually fragile. 

You might hit a target through sheer adrenaline, but you won't sustain a career.

I have kept his words with me ever since, saying them out loud in my head every time the scales started to tip unhealthily.

As the late business philosopher Jim Rohn famously said:

"Your personal life is a reflection of your professional success. If you want to have a better professional life, the answer is to have a better personal life."

In the rush to climb the ladder, it’s easy to neglect the home front. 

But the reality is that the boardroom doesn't build you up so you can face the living room; the living room builds you up so you can face the boardroom.

(photo credit: Susanna Milisic who always manages to capture my best self.)

posts.text:
"Who does what?" is maybe the simplest question, but the answers might surprise you. 🤔

Today, I facilitated a Role Alignment Workshop with one of my teams, and I have to share it because it was not only fun and inspiring, but incredibly eye-opening.

We often assume that job titles create automatic clarity. But in reality, perception gaps can lead to frustration, duplicated effort, or tasks falling through the cracks. So, we decided to map it out, literally.

The Setup:
We gathered the core roles in our team:

👤 Product Owner (PO)
🏗️ Solution Architect
💻 Solution Developer/Engineer
🛡️ Scrum Master
👥 Team Manager

The Format:
We grabbed a stack of differently colored Post-its and posed a simple question: "WHO DOES WHAT?" where "WHO" is the role at hand. This format was designed to help everyone structure their thoughts around specific expectations.

1. Write: Each team member spent 10 minutes writing down their perception of what each role is responsible for.
2. Share & Cluster: We went role by role. Each person read their Post-its aloud, and we placed them on the wall, clustering the common themes together.
3. Discuss Outliers: The real gold was in the outliers. Whenever a note didn't match the cluster, when someone saw a responsibility that others didn't, we paused and discussed it. Why did that perception exist? Was it a past experience, a hidden assumption, or a genuine gap in our ways of working?
4. Deep Dive: We spent about 10-15 minutes on each role, learning a massive amount about how we see ourselves and how our teammates see us.

The Result?
There were definitely a few laughs along the way (some perceptions were delightfully off-base), but the main takeaway was clarity. 

We walked away with a shared understanding of ownership, accountability, and collaboration points.

We also made sure to include our colleagues working from home.

They dictated their notes to us on the spot, and we pinned them up right alongside everyone else's. 

Inclusion means making sure every voice is physically represented on the wall, even if they aren't in the room.

We capped it off with a fika for those on-site (because no good workshop ends without coffee and pastries ☕️).

Why I recommend this:
If you have new team members, or if you just feel that "vibe" of misalignment creeping in, try this format. 

It’s low-tech, high-impact, and a great way to recalibrate expectations in a safe and visual way.

Big shoutout to my amazing team members for patiently agreeing to try out this format and make something great out of it!

Anna Mononen, Asad Ali, Sampath Kovuri, Utsav Bhattacharjee, Hassan Ahmed, Lukas Rådmann, Amal Deny, Mahesh Karumanchi, Xinfeng Li

#Agile #TeamAlignment #Leadership #Collaboration #Fika #RoleClarity

I’ve noticed that assessors tend to fall into one of two distinct categories: Enablers and Blockers.

They focus strictly on their own realm of assessment responsibility. They look at the initiative and provide the right pointers, where the risks and gaps truly lie, and what the team should focus on to move forward. Their goal is to guide the project toward success by illuminating the path.

🚧 The Blockers:
They tend to go all over the place, straying far beyond their own area of expertise. In the process, they don’t provide succinct or sufficient guidance regarding the actual risks or gaps. They raise noise, not signal. The result is confusion for the initiative, not clarity.

I’ve always prided myself on being an enabler rather than a blocker.

My mantra is simple: 

If you can’t help, don’t obstruct.

Experience isn’t just about knowing what can go wrong. 

It’s about knowing how to communicate those risks in a way that helps the other person succeed.

What type of committee member are you?

posts.text:
Throughout my 20-year career, I've observed the full spectrum of approaches to employee devices, from completely unmanaged to heavily restricted. 

Given the choice, I'd take the unmanaged approach over the excessively managed one, though I believe the optimal balance sits somewhere closer to the trust-based end of the scale.

What's interesting is what these approaches reveal about organisational culture. 

In unmanaged environments, trust in employees is essentially a given, regardless of it’s because managing devices is considered unnecessary or too expensive. 

In overly managed environments, trust as well as user centricity feels conspicuously absent, and research suggests this matters. 

Studies have found that perceived trust directly increases job performance, while intensive monitoring can negatively impact employee engagement.

I've observed an interesting dynamic at play: employees with unmanaged devices often demonstrate stronger security awareness. 

They know responsibility rests with them. 

Conversely, in heavily managed environments, people understandably assume security is someone else's problem somewhere else in the chain.

The employee experience cost is real too. 

When people spend considerable time working around device limitations just to do their jobs, productivity suffers. 

The numbers bear this out: research shows employees can lose an average of 36 workdays per year to IT roadblocks, with large enterprises losing over $100 million annually to digital inefficiencies.

It brings to mind the Jurassic Park quote: "life finds a way." 

When confronted with restrictions that hinder their work, employees will inevitably seek alternatives. 

This isn't just anecdotal: studies find that while IT departments believe the average enterprise users need around 37 applications, employees actually use over 600, including over 170 #AI apps, to get their work done. 

Inadequate IT resources and slow response times are consistently cited as the primary drivers of this shadow IT.

Over time, this friction contributes to quiet quitting. 

Survey data shows that a significant portion of employees say better technology tools would make them less likely to consider leaving their roles.

Striking the right balance between security and usability remains one of the more nuanced challenges in this field. 

Getting it right matters - for both trust and retention.

#EnterpriseIT #EmployeeExperience #CyberSecurity #WorkplaceCulture #ShadowIT #DigitalWorkplace

Our 3 Core Principles On How To Scale a Cloud Platform Team Without Scaling the Headcount

For years, our cloud foundation team has operated as a "one pizza team." 

Despite our size remaining more or less static, the number of users and environments we support has exploded, growing from a handful to thousands.

How did we manage that growth without burning out or bloating the team? 

It wasn’t magic, but a strict adherence to three key principles:

1️⃣ Everything as Code

We are religious about defining every single part of our platform as code. This isn't just about automation for speed; it’s about embedding control, repeatability, reusability, and traceability into our DNA. If it isn’t code, it isn’t part of the platform.

2️⃣ Favoring Native Services

We consciously choose to use the cloud provider's native services whenever possible. While third-party integrations can be tempting, they introduce new seams. By minimizing those seams, we keep the platform consistent and the management overhead surprisingly low.

3️⃣ Strict Boundary Management (The Hardest One)

In a cloud journey, it's easy for a platform team to compensate for others to "enable progress." We learned to be hyper-conscious of where our platform ends and where others must take over. Compensating for what others should be doing is a double-edged sword: it spikes your cognitive load and degrades the quality of your own deliveries.

By sticking to these principles, the governance and provisioning of environments has remained stable, even as usage skyrocketed.

However, success creates its own challenges. 

With thousands of users comes a significant increase in varied support needs. 

Automation is the ultimate solution here too, but it requires a specific pattern to emerge before we can build it. 

It’s a positive scaling problem to have, but a problem nonetheless.

Has your team faced the shift from "scaling infrastructure" to "scaling support"? I’d love to hear how you are handling it.

#CloudEngineering #PlatformEngineering #DevOps #Scaling #InfrastructureAsCode #CloudFoundation

I keep hearing versions of this:

"We need to know/control [X] in our cloud setup, so we need to buy, install, and run this third-party tool. We’ll need to connect it everywhere and grant it extensive privileges to get that visibility and control."

But here is the hard truth I see far too often:

If your default solution to a cloud challenge is to immediately go buy a third-party tool, you haven’t really understood how cloud works (yet).

The major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) are not just infrastructure hosts; they are operating systems for the data center. 

Much of the visibility and control you are chasing is either:

1. Available out of the box (plain old Billing or CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, Azure Monitor, IAM Insights etc.).

2. Incredibly easy to develop lightweight solutions for, using native SDKs and serverless functions.

Before you introduce another agent to manage, another bill to pay, and another set of "extensive privileges" to audit, ask the hard question:

"What can we do with the cloud tooling we already pay for?"

You might find the answer is already there. 

Don't let convenience tools mask a lack of cloud fluency.

Stop treating #Teams like it’s a phone or a walkie-talkie.

We’ve all adopted bad habits in the era of hybrid work. 

Here are my top 2 offenders (and how to fix them):

🚨 The Ad-Hoc Caller
You see the "Online" dot and hit "Call" immediately.

👎 Why it’s bad: The receiver might be in deep focus, in a meeting, or just simply doesn’t want to be disturbed.

✅ Fix: Send a quick ping first. "Hey, got 5 mins to talk about X?"


🚨 The Drive-By "Hi"
"Hi," ... (crickets) … (waits and waits for reply before typing the actual question).

👎 Why it’s bad: It assumes the other person is sitting there waiting for a live chat. It creates anxiety and context switching for the receiver.

✅ Fix: "Hi [Name], quick question about this [topic] when you have a sec."


Digital etiquette isn't about formalities. 

It's about respecting your colleagues’ time and attention span.

Over the years, I’ve found that seniority at work isn’t about age, experience, tenure or position.

Seniority at work is simply about navigating different organisational intricacies shrewdly and delivering results inspite of these organisational intricacies.

Here is what that definition truly implies about a person:

1. They are fluent in the "Shadow Structure"

They don't just read the organizational chart; they read the room. 

They understand that every company has two operating systems:

- The Official Structure: Titles, policies, reporting lines, and strategy decks.

- The Human Structure: Trust networks, informal influence, historical loyalties, and unspoken veto power.

Seniority is the ability to get the official work done by masterfully navigating the human structure.

2. They are pragmatists, not idealists

A less senior person might say, "The process says we need to do it this way, so we're stuck." 

A senior person sees the process not as a cage, but as a suggestion. 

They accept that "organisational intricacies" (politics, bureaucracy, red tape etc.) are a permanent feature of the landscape, not a bug to be fixed. 

They stop wishing for a perfect system and start winning within the imperfect one.

3. They are defined by "In spite of"

This is the most critical part of this thinking. 

Navigating intricacies shrewdly could just describe a skilled politician. But coupling it with delivering results separates the value-creators from the power-players.

- The politician navigates intricacies to elevate their own status.

- The senior professional navigates intricacies to deliver outcomes despite the friction.

In short:

A senior professional is someone who has mastered the art of execution despite entropy. 

He or she is a pragmatic realist who uses their understanding of the human and systemic landscape as a tool to build a path to the finish line.

posts.text:
Feb 11th 2001 - exactly 25 years ago - was the first day of a 3-day meeting at the Snowbird ski resort in Utah that gave birth to the Manifesto for Agile Software Development.

Like all important documents, most folks who think they know what it says don't appear to have actually read it.

There's no mention of "sprints", or "story points", or "user stories", or "build pipelines" or "Kanban boards" or any of the other details of specific Agile methodologies. The Manifesto isn't a "how-to" guide. It's a "why-to" guide.

It lays out a set of values and principles for teams who wish to be more responsive to changing user and business needs, through continuous learning and adaptation in a highly people-centric - and especially customer-centric - process.

On the front page of the manifesto's website, it states:

"We are uncovering better ways of developing software by doing it and helping others do it.

Through this work we have come to value:

- Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
- Working software over comprehensive documentation
- Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
- Responding to change over following a plan

That is, while there is value in the items on the right, we value the items on the left more."

The values and principles of Agile Software Development describe what it is like to *be* agile (with a small 'a'). We do not "do" agile. We do not "adopt" agile. We do not "implement" agile.

We *become* agile.

The manifesto itself is, of course, a laudable but compromised attempt to bring multiple iterative and "lightweight" development methodologies - different "brands" - under one banner.

One of those brands came to dominate in the minds of software developers. 

Another came to dominate in the minds of managers and executives, and ultimately came to dominate economically. This was a mistake.

"Agile Software Development" is doing software development in an agile way. It's not a management thing. 

Agile teams are self-organising, because putting decision-making power where (and more importantly, when) decisions need to be made - and therefore understood - is one of the keys to unlocking greater agility. A chain of command almost always becomes a serious bottleneck.

The other key message that got lost in translation is the need for strong technical chops. Teams who can't deliver rapidly, reliably and sustainably get micromanaged, and that ends their experiment in agility.

Without development teams with strong technical *discipline* who are empowered to make decisions, Agile Software Development becomes little more than Agility Theatre. And that's the reality for the majority of teams today - command-and-control waterfall development wearing an Agile Halloween costume.

I recommend reading the manifesto again (or for the first time). Perhaps do it as a team. Maybe talk about how the values and principles could or are helping, and identify where your organisation might just be play-acting

“The only way to go fast, is to go well.” - Robert C. Martin, "Clean Architecture"

In today’s fast moving (technology) world, success is not only about speed, innovation or the right product-market fit, it is also about standing on the right foundation.

A strong cloud foundation isn’t a “nice to have.” 

It’s the difference between an organisation that moves fast with confidence and one that struggles with security gaps, inconsistent environments, and operational friction.

Here’s a "short" sales pitch why a cloud foundation matters:

🔒 Security by Default
A well‑designed foundation enforces guardrails from day 1; identity, networking, logging, encryption, governance. Instead of reacting to risks, teams start from a secure baseline and build upward.

🎯 Consistency Across Environments
When environments are created through standardised landing zone templates and policies, you avoid configuration drift. Dev, test, and production behave predictably, lowering operational risk and simplifying troubleshooting.

⚙️ Scalability & Automation
A cloud foundation forces automation-first practices: Infrastructure as Code, CI/CD pipelines, micro‑segmented networks, and controlled service enablement. This turns the cloud into a repeatable, scalable platform instead of a collection of hand-crafted setups.

📉 Reduced Cost & Waste
Clear governance and cost controls, from budgets to approved services, keep cloud spend intentional rather than accidental. A good foundation doesn’t limit innovation; it channels it efficiently.

👩‍💻 Empowered Teams
When platform capabilities and boundaries are clear, teams can innovate faster. Developers get safe sandbox environments, product teams get clarity on what they can deploy, and security teams get full visibility and trust in the setup.

🌍 Compliance Made Achievable
Whether it’s data residency, auditing, or identity standards, a cloud foundation transforms compliance from a manual burden into an automated design principle.

A cloud foundation isn't just an IT construct, it's an accelerator for innovation, resilience, and trust.

A while back, I was in a later stage interview for a role I was fairly interested in. 

The recruiting manager spent 15 minutes of our 60-minute slot detailing his career, his achievements, and how tremendously well he had done.

That’s 25% of the available time, time meant for assessing fit, discussing the role, and addressing questions, used for a selfpromoting pitch.

It was an immediate red flag. 🚩 

It signaled a leadership style centered on self-promotion over mentorship, and a culture where listening might not be a priority.

I finished the interview professionally, but later excused myself from the process. 

I chose to look for leadership that lifts others up, not just themselves.

The takeaway: 

Interviews are a two-way street. 

They’re your window into team dynamics and leadership values. 

Pay close attention to how people in power use their time and share the platform.

When someone spends a quarter of an interview selling themselves to you, ask: 

What does that say about their focus? 

Their humility? 

Their capacity to develop others?

Culture fit is about spoken and nonspoken signals. 

And sometimes, the signal is a manager who forgets the interview isn’t about them.
